intraoperative and early postoperative pain we perform intercostal block in each intercostal space then the lung is retracted posteriorly and some adhesion between the lingula and the pericardium are released with the diathermic hook and the dissection starts on to the reflection of the mediastinal pleura with the visceral pleura and over the superior pulmonary vein

There is debate in the literature on the value of segmentectomy for lung cancer but when the lesion is entirely located in the upper proper lobe the trisegmentectomy such as a lingual sparing upper lobectomy on the left is widely accepted.
In order to divide the pulmonary vein we need to go distally until the confluence of the lingual vein
with the upper proper vane.

This is the exit point and then we will be ready to pass an instrument underneath and sew a staper.
The 12 mm staper can be used through the sub-siphoid port.

This is some connective tissue between the vein and the artery and the dissection of this tissue is of vital importance in order to go over the artery into the right plane and identify the arteries going to provide blood supply to the upper lobe.
The anatomy of the left pulmonary artery is the most variable, between 4-7 branches can
provide blood supply to the left upper lobe and in this case there were three providing blood supply to the upper probe.
The lung is retracted posteriorly and further dissection of the connective tissue and pleura around the artery is important in order to identify this apical posterior and anterior segment.

5 mm shaft device could go through the intercostal port at this stage, but a traditional 12 mm shaft stapler can be used with appropriate angle from the sub-siphoid port.
In fact, a variety of reports are published on sub-siphoid only surgery.

mistaken with A6, such as the apical segmental artery for the lower lobe, but now that we saw it into the fissure going in the upper lobe, we are sure that this is another posterior segmental branch and needs to be divided in order to complete the procedure.
